subject: Chemistry
q: A photon of energy $h\nu$ is absorbed by a free electron of a metal having work function $w < h\nu$. Then :
question: A photon of energy $h\nu$ is absorbed by a free electron of a metal having work function $w < h\nu$. Then :
options: The electron is sure to come out
The electron is sure to come out with a kinetic energy $(h\nu - w)$
Either the electron does not come out or it comes with a kinetic energy $(h\nu - w)$
It may come out with a kinetic energy less than $(h\nu - w)$
solution:
For photoelectric emission, the condition for an electron to be emitted is that the photon energy should be at least equal to the work function.

Here, we are given

$$ h\nu > w $$

So the photon has enough energy to remove an electron from the metal.

But in a metal, electrons are not all at the same energy level. Some electrons may be deeper inside the metal and may lose some energy before coming out at the surface. Because of this, the emitted electron is not always guaranteed to have kinetic energy exactly equal to $h\nu - w$.

The maximum kinetic energy is given by Einstein’s photoelectric equation:

$$ K_{\max} = h\nu - w $$

So an emitted electron can have kinetic energy less than this value also.

That is why:

  • Option A is not fully correct in the intended NCERT sense, because absorption by some free electron does not ensure that this particular electron definitely escapes.

  • Option B is wrong because $h\nu - w$ is the maximum kinetic energy, not always the actual kinetic energy.

  • Option C is wrong for the same reason.

  • Option D is correct.

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: It may come out with a kinetic energy less than } (h\nu - w)} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A 40 W electric bulb emits light of wavelength 620 nm and works with 80% efficiency. Find the number of photons emitted by the bulb in 20 seconds. (1 eV = $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J, $hc = 12400$ eV Å)
question: A 40 W electric bulb emits light of wavelength 620 nm and works with 80% efficiency. Find the number of photons emitted by the bulb in 20 seconds. (1 eV = $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J, $hc = 12400$ eV Å)
solution:
The bulb has a power of 40 W, but only 80% of it goes into producing light. So the useful power is

$$P = 0.8 \times 40 = 32 \text{ W}$$

In 20 seconds, the total light energy given out is

$$E = P \times t = 32 \times 20 = 640 \text{ J}$$

Now we need the energy of a single photon. The wavelength is 620 nm, which is $6200$ Å. Using the given relation,

$$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{12400}{6200} = 2 \text{ eV}$$

Converting this into joules,

$$E_{photon} = 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 3.2 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

The number of photons is just the total energy divided by the energy of one photon.

$$n = \frac{E}{E_{photon}} = \frac{640}{3.2 \times 10^{-19}} = 2 \times 10^{21}$$

So the bulb emits $2 \times 10^{21}$ photons in 20 seconds, which matches option D.

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider two atoms represented as $z_1A^{M_1}$ and $z_2B^{M_2}$. Given that $M_1 \neq M_2$ and $Z_1 \neq Z_2$, but the difference $(M_1 - Z_1)$ is equal to $(M_2 - Z_2)$, these two species are classified as
question: Consider two atoms represented as $z_1A^{M_1}$ and $z_2B^{M_2}$. Given that $M_1 \neq M_2$ and $Z_1 \neq Z_2$, but the difference $(M_1 - Z_1)$ is equal to $(M_2 - Z_2)$, these two species are classified as
solution:
Here $Z$ represents the atomic number, and $M$ represents the mass number.

We know that

$$ M_1 - Z_1 = M_2 - Z_2 $$

Now, $M - Z$ gives the number of neutrons, because

$$ \text{Number of neutrons} = \text{Mass number} - \text{Atomic number} $$

So the given condition means both elements have the same number of neutrons.

Also, it is given that

$$ M_1 \ne M_2 \quad \text{and} \quad Z_1 \ne Z_2 $$

So they are not the same element, but they have equal neutrons.

Elements having the same number of neutrons are called isotones.

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A: Isotonic}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: Moseley's equation for the determination of wavelength of X-rays is (v = frequency of wave, Z = nuclear charge, a and b are constants)
question: Moseley's equation for the determination of wavelength of X-rays is (v = frequency of wave, Z = nuclear charge, a and b are constants)
solution:
Moseley studied the X-rays given out by different elements and noticed a neat pattern. When he plotted the square root of the frequency of the X-rays against the nuclear charge $Z$, he got a straight line.

A straight line means the relation must be of the form $\sqrt{v} = (\text{slope})(Z) + (\text{intercept})$. Writing this in the standard way with constants $a$ and $b$, the relation becomes

$$\sqrt{v} = a(Z - b)$$

Here $a$ is the slope of the line and $b$ is a small correction constant (called the screening constant). The square root sign on $v$ is important, because that is exactly what made the graph come out as a straight line.

So the correct choice is Option C.

subject: Chemistry
q: Light of wavelength $\lambda$ shines on a metal surface with intensity $X$, and the metal emits $Y$ electrons per second of average energy $Z$. What will happen to $Y$ and $Z$ if $X$ is doubled?
question: Light of wavelength $\lambda$ shines on a metal surface with intensity $X$, and the metal emits $Y$ electrons per second of average energy $Z$. What will happen to $Y$ and $Z$ if $X$ is doubled?
options: $Y$ will be doubled and $Z$ will become half.
$Y$ will remain same and $Z$ will be doubled.
$Y$ will be doubled but $Z$ will remain same.
solution:
The correct answer is Option D.

When light falls on a metal surface, electrons get emitted. This is the photoelectric effect. Here we need to look at two separate things: how many electrons come out per second, and how much energy each electron carries.

The intensity $X$ tells us how much light energy is hitting the surface every second. If we keep the wavelength $\lambda$ the same but double the intensity, it means twice as many photons are striking the metal each second.

Now, the number of electrons emitted depends on how many photons hit the surface. Each photon can knock out one electron. So if the number of photons becomes double, the number of emitted electrons per second also becomes double. That means $Y$ gets doubled.

But what about the energy of each electron? The energy of a single emitted electron depends on the energy of each photon, which is given by

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

This energy depends only on the wavelength, not on the intensity. Since the wavelength $\lambda$ has not changed, the energy of each photon stays the same. So the average energy $Z$ of the emitted electrons does not change at all.

This is an important idea in the photoelectric effect. Increasing intensity gives more electrons but does not make them more energetic. To change their energy, you would have to change the wavelength.

So when $X$ is doubled, $Y$ becomes double and $Z$ stays the same.

subject: Chemistry
q: In a photoelectric effect setup, silver metal emits photoelectrons when light of wavelength 256.7 nm falls on it. It is found that applying a stopping potential of 0.35 V just prevents the photoelectrons from reaching the collector. Determine the work function of silver.
question: In a photoelectric effect setup, silver metal emits photoelectrons when light of wavelength 256.7 nm falls on it. It is found that applying a stopping potential of 0.35 V just prevents the photoelectrons from reaching the collector. Determine the work function of silver.
solution:
For photoelectric effect, the main relation is

$$ h\nu=\phi+K_{\max} $$

Here, $\phi$ is the work function and $K_{\max}$ is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons.

Since the stopping potential is $0.35\ \text{V}$,

$$ K_{\max}=eV_0=0.35\ \text{eV} $$

Now find the energy of the incident radiation using

$$ E=\frac{hc}{\lambda} $$

For wavelength $\lambda=256.7\ \text{nm}$,

$$ E=\frac{1240}{256.7}\ \text{eV} $$

$$ E \approx 4.83\ \text{eV} $$

Now,

$$ \phi = E - K_{\max} $$

$$ \phi = 4.83 - 0.35 $$

$$ \phi = 4.48\ \text{eV} $$

So, the work function of silver is

$$ \boxed{4.48\ \text{eV}} $$

Correct option: C

subject: Chemistry
q: The radius of a hydrogen atom is approximately 0.05 nm, while the radius of its proton (nucleus) is $1.5 \times 10^{-15}$ m. Treating both the hydrogen atom and the proton as spheres, find the fraction of the total volume of a hydrogen atom that is occupied by its nucleus.
question: The radius of a hydrogen atom is approximately 0.05 nm, while the radius of its proton (nucleus) is $1.5 \times 10^{-15}$ m. Treating both the hydrogen atom and the proton as spheres, find the fraction of the total volume of a hydrogen atom that is occupied by its nucleus.
solution:
The fraction of space occupied by the nucleus is the ratio of the volume of the nucleus to the volume of the atom.

Since both are spherical,

$$ \text{Fraction occupied}=\frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi r_n^3}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r_a^3} =\left(\frac{r_n}{r_a}\right)^3 $$

Here,

$$ r_a = 0.05\ \text{nm} = 0.05 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m} $$

and

$$ r_n = 1.5 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{m} $$

Now,

$$ \frac{r_n}{r_a}=\frac{1.5\times 10^{-15}}{5\times 10^{-11}} =0.3\times 10^{-4} =3\times 10^{-5} $$

So,

$$ \text{Fraction} = (3\times 10^{-5})^3 =27\times 10^{-15} =2.7\times 10^{-14} $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{2.7\times 10^{-14}} $$

Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q: Each Br2 molecule in the gaseous state absorbs one quantum of light to dissociate into Br atoms. If the absorbed radiation has wavelength 5000 Å, find the energy needed in kJ/mol.
question: Each Br2 molecule in the gaseous state absorbs one quantum of light to dissociate into Br atoms. If the absorbed radiation has wavelength 5000 Å, find the energy needed in kJ/mol.
solution:
The energy of one quantum of light depends on its wavelength. We use the relation

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Here $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$, and the wavelength is

$$\lambda = 5000\ \text{Å} = 5000 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$$

So the energy absorbed by one molecule is

$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.9756 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

Since one quantum is absorbed per molecule, for one mole we multiply by Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$.

$$E = 3.9756 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

$$E = 2.394 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/mol}$$

Converting into kilojoules,

$$E \approx 240\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

So the correct answer is option A.

subject: Chemistry
q: Photoelectric emission is observed from two surfaces at incident frequencies $ u_1$ and $ u_2$ ($ u_1 > u_2$). Maximum kinetic energies of the photoelectrons emitted in the above two cases are in the ratio of $1 : k$. If threshold frequency in both cases is $ u_0$, then this value is
question: Photoelectric emission is observed from two surfaces at incident frequencies $ u_1$ and $ u_2$ ($ u_1 > u_2$). Maximum kinetic energies of the photoelectrons emitted in the above two cases are in the ratio of $1 : k$. If threshold frequency in both cases is $ u_0$, then this value is
solution:
For photoelectric emission, Einstein’s photoelectric equation is

$$ K_{\max} = h( u - u_0) $$

Here, the threshold frequency is the same in both cases, so

$$ K_1 = h( u_1 - u_0) $$

and

$$ K_2 = h( u_2 - u_0) $$

Given that the maximum kinetic energies are in the ratio $1:k$, we can write

$$ rac{K_1}{K_2} = rac{1}{k} $$

So,

$$ rac{h( u_1 - u_0)}{h( u_2 - u_0)} = rac{1}{k} $$

The $h$ cancels:

$$ rac{ u_1 - u_0}{ u_2 - u_0} = rac{1}{k} $$

Cross-multiplying,

$$ k( u_1 - u_0) = u_2 - u_0 $$

$$ k u_1 - k u_0 = u_2 - u_0 $$

Bring the $ u_0$ terms together:

$$ k u_1 - u_2 = k u_0 - u_0 $$

$$ k u_1 - u_2 = (k-1) u_0 $$

So,

$$ u_0 = rac{k u_1 - u_2}{k-1} $$

So the correct option is

$$ oxed{ ext{Option B}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A light source of wavelength $\lambda$ illuminates a metal and ejects photo-electrons with $(K.E.)_{\text{max}} = 1 \text{ eV}$

Another light source of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{3}$ ejects photo-electrons from same metal with $(K.E.)_{\text{max}} = 4 \text{ eV}$

Find the value of work function?
question:
A light source of wavelength $\lambda$ illuminates a metal and ejects photo-electrons with $(K.E.)_{\text{max}} = 1 \text{ eV}$

Another light source of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{3}$ ejects photo-electrons from same metal with $(K.E.)_{\text{max}} = 4 \text{ eV}$

Find the value of work function?
solution:
Use Einstein’s photoelectric equation:

$$ K_{\max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi $$

For the first light source of wavelength $\lambda$:

$$ 1 = \frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi $$

For the second light source of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{3}$, the frequency becomes $3$ times, so photon energy becomes:

$$ \frac{hc}{\lambda/3} = \frac{3hc}{\lambda} $$

Now using the photoelectric equation again:

$$ 4 = \frac{3hc}{\lambda} - \phi $$

Now subtract the first equation from the second:

$$ 4 - 1 = \left(\frac{3hc}{\lambda} - \phi\right) - \left(\frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi\right) $$

$$ 3 = \frac{2hc}{\lambda} $$

$$ \frac{hc}{\lambda} = 1.5 \text{ eV} $$

Put this in the first equation:

$$ 1 = 1.5 - \phi $$

$$ \phi = 0.5 \text{ eV} $$

So the work function is:

$$ \boxed{0.5 \text{ eV}} $$

Correct option: C

subject: Chemistry
q: Light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on a metal surface and the emitted photoelectrons have a maximum kinetic energy of 1 eV. When another light of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{3}$ falls on the same metal, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons becomes 4 eV. The work function of the metal is to be written in the form $x \times 10^{-1}\,\mathrm{eV}$, where $x$ is
question: Light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on a metal surface and the emitted photoelectrons have a maximum kinetic energy of 1 eV. When another light of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{3}$ falls on the same metal, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons becomes 4 eV. The work function of the metal is to be written in the form $x \times 10^{-1}\,\mathrm{eV}$, where $x$ is
solution:
Use Einstein’s photoelectric equation:

$$K_{\max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi$$

For the first radiation of wavelength $\lambda$,

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi = 1 \text{ eV}$$

For the second radiation of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{3}$, the photon energy becomes

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda/3} = \frac{3hc}{\lambda}$$

So,

$$\frac{3hc}{\lambda} - \phi = 4 \text{ eV}$$

Now subtract the first equation from the second:

$$\left(\frac{3hc}{\lambda} - \phi\right) - \left(\frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi\right) = 4 - 1$$

$$\frac{2hc}{\lambda} = 3$$

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ eV}$$

Put this in the first equation:

$$1.5 - \phi = 1$$

$$\phi = 0.5 \text{ eV}$$

Now write it in the form $x \times 10^{-1}\,\text{eV}$:

$$0.5 \text{ eV} = 5 \times 10^{-1} \text{ eV}$$

So, $x = 5$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
An electron starts from rest and is accelerated through a potential difference of 40 kV. Its de Broglie wavelength is $x \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. Find the value of $x$ (Nearest integer).

Given: Mass of electron $=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$

Charge of an electron $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$

Planck constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}$
question:
An electron starts from rest and is accelerated through a potential difference of 40 kV. Its de Broglie wavelength is $x \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. Find the value of $x$ (Nearest integer).

Given: Mass of electron $=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$

Charge of an electron $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$

Planck constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}$
solution:
When an electron is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the work done on it becomes its kinetic energy. So we can write:

$$eV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

From this, the momentum of the electron comes out as:

$$p = mv = \sqrt{2meV}$$

The de Broglie wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$$

Now let's put in the values. Here $V = 40\,\text{kV} = 40000\,\text{V}$.

First find the quantity under the root:

$$2meV = 2 \times (9.1 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times 40000$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$2 \times 9.1 \times 1.6 = 29.12$$

So we have:

$$29.12 \times 10^{-31-19} \times 4 \times 10^{4} = 116.48 \times 10^{-46}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\sqrt{116.48 \times 10^{-46}} = \sqrt{116.48} \times 10^{-23} \approx 10.79 \times 10^{-23}$$

Now the wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{10.79 \times 10^{-23}}$$

$$\lambda \approx 0.614 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m} = 6.14 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}$$

So the value of $x$ is about $6$.

$$\boxed{x = 6}$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A monochromatic light source of wavelength 400 nm emits 1000 J energy in 10 s. This radiation is incident on a sodium surface and causes emission of $x \times 10^{20}$ electrons per second. Take that 400 nm radiation has enough energy to just produce photoelectrons from sodium. The value of $x$ is

$\_\_\_\_$ . (Nearest integer) $\left(h=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)$
question:
A monochromatic light source of wavelength 400 nm emits 1000 J energy in 10 s. This radiation is incident on a sodium surface and causes emission of $x \times 10^{20}$ electrons per second. Take that 400 nm radiation has enough energy to just produce photoelectrons from sodium. The value of $x$ is

$\_\_\_\_$ . (Nearest integer) $\left(h=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)$
solution:
Energy supplied per second is

$$ \frac{1000}{10} = 100\ \text{J s}^{-1} $$

Now, energy of one photon of wavelength $400\ \text{nm}$ is

$$ E=\frac{hc}{\lambda} $$

Given,

$$ h=6.626\times 10^{-34}\ \text{Js}, \quad c=3\times 10^8\ \text{m s}^{-1}, \quad \lambda=400\times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} $$

So,

$$ E=\frac{6.626\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^8}{400\times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ E=4.97\times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} $$

This is the energy of one photon.

Number of photons falling per second is

$$ \frac{100}{4.97\times 10^{-19}} =2.01\times 10^{20} $$

Since one photon can eject one electron, number of electrons ejected per second is also

$$ 2.01\times 10^{20} $$

So,

$$ x \approx 2 $$

Hence, the nearest integer value of $x$ is

$$ \boxed{2} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Radiation with a wavelength of 500 nm falls on a metallic surface. If the threshold frequency required for the photoelectric effect to occur on this metal is $4.3 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$, determine the speed of the emitted photoelectron as $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.

(Round off to the nearest integer)

(Given: Planck's constant $h=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}$, mass of electron $m_e=9.0 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$)
question:
Radiation with a wavelength of 500 nm falls on a metallic surface. If the threshold frequency required for the photoelectric effect to occur on this metal is $4.3 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$, determine the speed of the emitted photoelectron as $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.

(Round off to the nearest integer)

(Given: Planck's constant $h=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}$, mass of electron $m_e=9.0 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$)
solution:
For photoelectric emission, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron is

$$ K_{\max} = h\nu - h\nu_0 = h(\nu - \nu_0) $$

Given wavelength,

$$ \lambda = 500\ \text{nm} = 500 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} $$

So,

$$ \nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{500 \times 10^{-9}} = 6 \times 10^{14}\ \text{Hz} $$

Threshold frequency is

$$ \nu_0 = 4.3 \times 10^{14}\ \text{Hz} $$

Now,

$$ K_{\max} = h(\nu - \nu_0) = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times (6 - 4.3)\times 10^{14} $$

$$ = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 1.7 \times 10^{14} $$

$$ = 11.271 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J} $$

For the electron,

$$ K_{\max} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 $$

So,

$$ \frac{1}{2}(9.0 \times 10^{-31})v^2 = 11.271 \times 10^{-20} $$

$$ v^2 = \frac{2 \times 11.271 \times 10^{-20}}{9.0 \times 10^{-31}} $$

$$ v^2 = 2.5047 \times 10^{11} $$

$$ v = \sqrt{2.5047 \times 10^{11}} \approx 5.0 \times 10^5\ \text{m s}^{-1} $$

So the required value is

$$ \boxed{5} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: The work function for a metal is 40 eV . To emit photo electrons of zero velocity from the surface of the metal the wavelength of incident light should be x nm .
question: The work function for a metal is 40 eV . To emit photo electrons of zero velocity from the surface of the metal the wavelength of incident light should be x nm .
solution:
For photoelectrons to be emitted with zero velocity, the incident light must just supply the work function.

So we use

$$ h\nu = \phi $$

Since $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$,

$$ \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \phi $$

Now use the convenient value

$$ hc = 1240 \ \text{eV nm} $$

Given work function,

$$ \phi = 40 \ \text{eV} $$

So,

$$ \lambda = \frac{1240}{40} \ \text{nm} $$

$$ \lambda = 31 \ \text{nm} $$

So, $x = 31$.
